# California: formation requirements

## 1. Form a California nonprofit public benefit corporation

For a 501(c)(3) in California, the usual entity is a `Nonprofit Public Benefit Corporation`.

Official California Secretary of State form/instructions:

- ARTS-PB-501(c)(3) instructions PDF: https://bpd.cdn.sos.ca.gov/corp/pdf/articles/arts-pb.pdf
- California nonprofit public benefit corporation law overview: https://leginfo.legislature.ca.gov/faces/codes_displayexpandedbranch.xhtml?tocCode=CORP&division=2.&title=1.&part=2.

The California Secretary of State instructions state:

- use this form for corporations seeking federal 501(c)(3) status;
- filing fee for the articles is `$30`.

## 2. What California articles must include

California Corporations Code section 5130 requires the articles to include:

- corporate name;
- statement that it is a nonprofit public benefit corporation not organized for private gain;
- California agent for service of process;
- initial street address;
- initial mailing address, if different.

Source:

- Corp. Code § 5130: https://leginfo.legislature.ca.gov/faces/codes_displaySection.xhtml?lawCode=CORP&sectionNum=5130.

For a 501(c)(3), the practical filing approach is to use the California SOS 501(c)(3)-specific public benefit form so the IRS-friendly purpose and dedication concepts are built in.

## 3. Initial Statement of Information

After formation, California nonprofit corporations must file `Form SI-100`.

Official sources:

- Secretary of State FAQ: https://www.sos.ca.gov/business-programs/business-entities/faqs
- SOS welcome letter summary PDF: https://bpd.cdn.sos.ca.gov/bizfile/welcome-letter-corp.pdf

Timing:

- initial filing within `90 days` of registration;
- then every `2 years` in the applicable filing window based on registration date.

Current fee references on SOS pages indicate `SI-100` is generally `$20`.

## 4. Attorney General charitable registration

California charitable corporations must register with the Attorney General's Registry of Charities and Fundraisers.

Official page:

- Initial registration: https://oag.ca.gov/charities/initial-reg
- Paper filing page: https://oag.ca.gov/charities/initial-reg-paper

Timing:

- register within `30 days` of first receiving charitable assets.

Current AG page says the initial registration fee is `$50`.

The AG page says to submit:

- founding documents;
- current bylaws;
- IRS determination letter, if received;
- copy of Form 1023 / 1023-EZ / 1024 if filed;
- FEIN if IRS determination is still pending.

## 5. California state tax exemption

California tax exemption is not automatic just because the corporation is nonprofit.

Official FTB sources:

- FTB 3500A instructions: https://www.ftb.ca.gov/forms/misc/3500A-instructions.html
- FTB 3500 booklet: https://www.ftb.ca.gov/forms/misc/3500-booklet.html

Practical rule:

- if the IRS has already approved 501(c)(3) status, usually submit `FTB 3500A` with the federal determination letter;
- if no federal determination letter exists yet and state exemption is needed, use `FTB 3500`.

Important FTB point:

- California entities remain taxable until the FTB grants exempt status.

The FTB eliminated the prior application fee for exempt organization applications.

Source:

- FTB bulletin on fee elimination: https://www.ftb.ca.gov/about-ftb/newsroom/public-service-bulletins/2020-32-tax-exempt-organization-application-fee-and-filing-fees-eliminated.html

## 6. Practical California order of operations

Recommended order:

1. Reserve/confirm the name if needed.
2. File `ARTS-PB-501(c)(3)` with the California Secretary of State.
3. Adopt bylaws and appoint the initial board/officers.
4. Get EIN.
5. File `SI-100` within 90 days.
6. File IRS Form 1023 or 1023-EZ.
7. Register with the California Attorney General within 30 days of receiving charitable assets.
8. After federal approval, file `FTB 3500A` with the IRS determination letter.
